operating_state: RTX generated $68.9 billion in net sales for 2023, up $1.8 billion driven by organic growth across all segments from commercial aftermarket recovery, substantially offset by a $5.4 billion net sales charge related to powder metal contamination in PW1100 GTF engines powering A320neo aircraft requiring accelerated fleet inspections and creating elevated aircraft on ground levels expected through 2026. The company restructured from four to three business segments effective July 1, 2023, and changed its legal name from Raytheon Technologies Corporation effective July 17, 2023.
cost_structure: Operating profit margin compressed to 5.2% from 8.2% in 2022, primarily due to the $2.9 billion powder metal charge at Pratt & Whitney and negative EAC adjustments. Total cost of sales increased to $56.8 billion representing 82% of net sales, with organic cost increases from higher volumes partially offset by cost reductions. Fixed-price contracts expose the company to cost overrun risks from inflation, supply chain pressures, and material shortages constraining pricing flexibility.
material_events: RTX executed a $10 billion accelerated share repurchase program in October 2023, funded through long-term debt issuance and term loan facilities, receiving initial delivery of 108.45 million shares. S&P downgraded the credit rating from A-/negative to BBB+/stable in August 2023, with both S&P and Moody's shifting outlook to negative following the ASR announcement. Christopher Calio was elected President and CEO effective May 2, 2024, succeeding Gregory Hayes who becomes Executive Chairman.
macro_exposures: Supply chain disruptions continue impacting raw materials, microelectronics, and commodity procurement due to market constraints, inflation, and labor shortages, with effects expected to persist into 2024. Geopolitical tensions including Russia-Ukraine conflict and China sanctions against Raytheon Missiles & Defense create ongoing operational uncertainties, while cybersecurity threats have intensified from nation states and cyber criminals targeting defense contractors. The company faces significant U.S. government defense spending exposure under continuing resolution funding with potential sequestration risks.
forward_outlook: Management expects approximately $1.3 billion cash outflows in 2024 related to powder metal matter resolution, with significant incremental shop visits and elevated aircraft on ground levels through 2026 as part of the fleet management plan. The company maintains total backlog up from prior year, indicating strong future performance potential. RTX continues multi-year digital transformation initiatives and structural cost reduction efforts while pursuing pending divestitures to reduce outstanding debt.
uncertainty_profile: The powder metal issue involves significant estimation uncertainty regarding shop visit timing, inspection results, turnaround times, parts availability, and customer negotiation outcomes that could materially affect the $2.8 billion customer compensation accrual. Ongoing DOJ investigation into contract pricing matters continues with prior accrual, while various U.S. government audits could result in administrative, civil, or criminal liabilities. RTX faces potential significant tax liabilities if IRS determines post-separation acquisitions constitute part of a plan resulting in ownership changes triggering taxable gain.
